section_drafts.title: Positive Views on Generative AI's Impact on Employment
section_drafts.focus: Productivity enhancement, labor augmentation, complementarity effects, and new task creation driven by generative AI, drawing on seminal automation-and-reinstatement theory and early experimental productivity evidence.
section_drafts.verified_text: Autor argues that automation simultaneously substitutes for and complements labor, with productivity improvements in one set of tasks increasing the economic value of the remaining tasks that workers perform, a mechanism captured by the O-ring production function where making one link in a production chain more reliable raises the value of improving all other links.[1] In a study of generative AI adoption, the average user saved 5.4% of work hours, equivalent to 2.2 hours per week for a 40-hour worker, and when nonusers were included the figure fell to 1.4% of total hours saved because of generative AI.[2][3][4] By industry, information services led with 14.0% of work hours spent using generative AI and 2.6% time savings, while leisure, accommodation, and other services had the lowest figures at 2.3% and 0.6% respectively.[2][3][4] In a study of AI deployment, once individual agent and tenure fixed effects were added, the estimated effect of AI deployment on resolutions per hour settled at 0.30 chats, or 13.8%.[5][6][7][8] The typical customer chat in this study shortened by 3.8 minutes, a 9% reduction from the baseline.[5][6][7][8] Agents in this study gained the ability to handle 0.37 additional chats per hour, roughly a 14% increase over a baseline mean of 2.6.[5][6][7][8] Resolution rates in this study ticked up by 1.3 percentage points, significant at the 10% level, against an already high baseline of 82%.[5][6][7][8] Net promoter scores in this study showed no meaningful change, with a coefficient of -0.13 percentage points compared to a mean of 79.6%.[5][6][7][8] Generative AI influences labor through both automation, which substitutes technology for human tasks, and augmentation, which complements human skills.[9]

section_drafts.title: Negative Views on Generative AI's Impact on Employment
section_drafts.focus: Job displacement risks, occupational exposure to automation, labor market polarization, wage inequality, and evidence of short-term displacement effects from generative AI adoption.
section_drafts.verified_text: Most Americans expect generative artificial intelligence to exert a largely negative effect on employment over the coming two decades, according to a Pew Research Center poll that found widespread public concern about the technology's consequences for jobs and livelihoods.[1][2][3] The ILO's global study reports that 5.5 percent of total employment in high-income countries is potentially exposed to automation effects from generative AI, compared with only 0.4 per cent in low-income countries, a difference the authors attribute to varying occupational structures across income levels.[4] A new measure called observed exposure combines theoretical large language model capability with real-world usage data, weighting automated and work-related uses more heavily, and it finds that AI's actual coverage remains only a fraction of what is theoretically feasible.[5][6] Displacement effects intensify over time, with the coefficient for high-substitution occupations deepening from -0.155* to -0.064* in the first year post-release, -0.252*** to -0.135** in the second year, and -0.310*** to -0.182*** in the third year.[7][8][9] A formal pre-trend test with a Wald statistic of 0.83 and a p-value of 0.66 fails to reject the null hypothesis of common pre-trends, validating the identification strategy.[7][8][9]

payload.assembled_report_md:
# I am researching the impact of Generative AI on the future labor market, please help me complete a research report.

## Positive Views on Generative AI's Impact on Employment

Autor argues that automation simultaneously substitutes for and complements labor, with productivity improvements in one set of tasks increasing the economic value of the remaining tasks that workers perform, a mechanism captured by the O-ring production function where making one link in a production chain more reliable raises the value of improving all other links.[1] In a study of generative AI adoption, the average user saved 5.4% of work hours, equivalent to 2.2 hours per week for a 40-hour worker, and when nonusers were included the figure fell to 1.4% of total hours saved because of generative AI.[2][3][4] By industry, information services led with 14.0% of work hours spent using generative AI and 2.6% time savings, while leisure, accommodation, and other services had the lowest figures at 2.3% and 0.6% respectively.[2][3][4] In a study of AI deployment, once individual agent and tenure fixed effects were added, the estimated effect of AI deployment on resolutions per hour settled at 0.30 chats, or 13.8%.[5][6][7][8] The typical customer chat in this study shortened by 3.8 minutes, a 9% reduction from the baseline.[5][6][7][8] Agents in this study gained the ability to handle 0.37 additional chats per hour, roughly a 14% increase over a baseline mean of 2.6.[5][6][7][8] Resolution rates in this study ticked up by 1.3 percentage points, significant at the 10% level, against an already high baseline of 82%.[5][6][7][8] Net promoter scores in this study showed no meaningful change, with a coefficient of -0.13 percentage points compared to a mean of 79.6%.[5][6][7][8] Generative AI influences labor through both automation, which substitutes technology for human tasks, and augmentation, which complements human skills.[9]

## Negative Views on Generative AI's Impact on Employment

Most Americans expect generative artificial intelligence to exert a largely negative effect on employment over the coming two decades, according to a Pew Research Center poll that found widespread public concern about the technology's consequences for jobs and livelihoods.[1][2][3] The ILO's global study reports that 5.5 percent of total employment in high-income countries is potentially exposed to automation effects from generative AI, compared with only 0.4 per cent in low-income countries, a difference the authors attribute to varying occupational structures across income levels.[4] A new measure called observed exposure combines theoretical large language model capability with real-world usage data, weighting automated and work-related uses more heavily, and it finds that AI's actual coverage remains only a fraction of what is theoretically feasible.[5][6] Displacement effects intensify over time, with the coefficient for high-substitution occupations deepening from -0.155* to -0.064* in the first year post-release, -0.252*** to -0.135** in the second year, and -0.310*** to -0.182*** in the third year.[7][8][9] A formal pre-trend test with a Wald statistic of 0.83 and a p-value of 0.66 fails to reject the null hypothesis of common pre-trends, validating the identification strategy.[7][8][9]
